text:
The Camel Trader of Babylon

The hungrier you become, the clearer your mind works—and the more sensitive you become to the smell of food.

Tarkad, the son of Azure, certainly thought so. For two whole days, he hadn't eaten anything except two small figs he stole from a garden wall. He couldn't grab another before an angry woman rushed out and chased him down the street. Her shrill cries were still ringing in his ears as he walked through the marketplace. The memory of her voice helped him stop his restless fingers from snatching tempting fruit from the market women's baskets.

Never before had he realized how much food was brought to the markets of Babylon, or how good it smelled. Leaving the market, he walked across to the inn and paced back and forth in front of the eating house. Perhaps here he might meet someone he knew. He hoped to find someone who could lend him a copper coin. That single coin would win him a smile from the unfriendly innkeeper and a generous helping of food. Without the copper, he knew all too well how unwelcome he would be.

Lost in thought, he unexpectedly found himself face to face with the one man he wished most to avoid. It was the tall, bony figure of Dabasir, the camel trader. Of all the friends and acquaintances from whom he had borrowed small amounts of money, Dabasir made him feel the most uncomfortable. This was because Tarkad had failed to keep his promises to repay the money promptly.

Dabasir's face lit up when he saw him.

Ha! It is Tarkad! You are exactly the person I've been looking for. I want you to repay the two copper coins I lent you a month ago. I also want the silver coin I lent you before that. We are lucky to meet today. I can really use those coins right now. What do you say, boy? What do you say?
Tarkad stuttered and his face flushed red. He had nothing in his empty stomach to give him the courage to argue with the outspoken Dabasir.
I am sorry, very sorry,
he mumbled weakly,
but today I don't have the copper or the silver to repay you.
Then get it!
Dabasir insisted.
Surely you can get your hands on a few coppers and a piece of silver. You need to repay the generosity of your father's old friend who helped you when you were in need!
It is because bad luck follows me that I cannot pay.

Bad luck! Would you blame the gods for your own weakness? Bad luck chases every man who thinks more about borrowing than repaying. Come with me, boy, while I eat. I am hungry, and I want to tell you a story.

Tarkad flinched from Dabasir's brutal honesty. However, this was at least an invitation to enter the coveted doorway of the eating house.

Dabasir pushed him to a far corner of the room, where they sat down on small rugs.

When Kauskor, the proprietor, appeared with a smile, Dabasir addressed him with his usual casual manner.

Fat lizard of the desert, bring me a leg of goat, browned with plenty of juice! Bring bread and all the vegetables, because I am hungry and want a lot of food. Don't forget my friend here. Bring him a jug of water. Make sure it is cold, because it is a hot day.

Tarkad's heart sank. Did he have to sit here and drink water while watching this man eat an entire goat leg? He said nothing. He couldn't think of anything to say.

Dabasir, however, didn't know how to stay quiet. Smiling and waving good-naturedly to the other customers, who all knew him, he continued talking.

I heard a story from a traveler who just returned from Urfa. He spoke of a rich man who owns a piece of stone cut so thin that you can look right through it. He put it in the window of his house to keep out the rain. The traveler said the stone is yellow. When he was allowed to look through it, the entire outside world looked strange and different from reality. What do you say to that, Tarkad? Do you think the whole world could look like a completely different color to a man?
I suppose it could,
the young man replied. But Tarkad was much more interested in the fat goat leg that had just been placed in front of Dabasir.

Well, I know it is true because I myself have seen the world in a completely different color from what it really is. The story I am about to tell you is about how I managed to see the world in its true colors once again.
Dabasir is going to tell a story,

a nearby diner whispered to his neighbor, dragging his rug closer. Other diners brought their food and crowded around in a semi-circle. They chewed loudly in Tarkad's ears and brushed against him with their meaty bones. Tarkad was the only one without food. Dabasir didn't offer to share with him. He didn't even point to a small piece of hard bread that had broken off the platter and fallen to the floor.
The story I am about to tell,
began Dabasir, pausing to bite a huge chunk of meat from the goat leg,
is about my early life and how I became a camel trader. Did anyone here know that I was once a slave in Syria?
A murmur of surprise rippled through the audience. Dabasir listened to it with satisfaction.
When I was a young man,
Dabasir continued, after another fierce bite of the goat leg,
I learned my father's trade of saddle-making. I worked with him in his shop and got married.

Because I was young and not very skilled, I earned very little. It was barely enough to support my wonderful wife in a modest way. I wanted nice things that I couldn't afford. Soon, I discovered that shopkeepers would let me buy things on credit, trusting me to pay them back later even if I didn't have the money at the time.

Being young and inexperienced, I didn't realize the danger. A person who spends more than he earns is planting the seeds of unnecessary self-indulgence, and he is guaranteed to harvest a storm of trouble and humiliation. So, I gave in to my desires for fine clothing. I bought luxury items for my good wife and our home that we simply couldn't afford.

I paid what I could, and for a while, everything was fine. But eventually, I realized I couldn't stretch my earnings to cover both my living expenses and my debts. Creditors began chasing me down, demanding payment for my extravagant purchases. My life became miserable. I borrowed from my friends, but I couldn't repay them either. Things went from bad to worse. My wife went back to live with her father. I decided to leave Babylon and look for a new city where a young man might have better opportunities.

For two years, I lived a restless and unsuccessful life, working for caravan traders. Then, I fell in with a group of likable robbers who roamed the desert, attacking unarmed caravans. Such actions were completely beneath a son of my honorable father. But I was seeing the world through a colored stone, and I didn't realize how far I had fallen.
We were successful on our first trip. We captured a rich haul of gold, silks, and valuable merchandise. We took this loot to Ginir and quickly wasted it all.

The second time, we weren't so lucky. Right after we had captured a caravan, we were attacked by the spearmen of a local tribal chief. The caravans paid him for protection. Our two leaders were killed, and the rest of us were taken to Damascus. There, they stripped us of our clothing and sold us as slaves.

I was bought for two silver coins by a Syrian desert chief. With my hair shaved off and wearing nothing but a loincloth, I didn't look any different from the other slaves. Because I was a reckless young man, I thought it was just an adventure. That changed when my master brought me before his four wives and told them they could have me as a eunuch.
Then, I truly realized the complete hopelessness of my situation. These desert men were fierce warriors. I was completely at their mercy, with no weapons and no way to escape.

I stood there in fear as those four women looked me over. I wondered if I could expect any pity from them. Sira, the first wife, was older than the rest. Her face showed no emotion as she stared at me. I looked away from her, finding little comfort. The next wife was an arrogant beauty who looked at me as if I were a worm in the dirt. The two younger wives giggled, acting like the whole situation was an exciting joke.
It felt like an eternity as I stood there waiting for my sentence. Each woman seemed to want the others to decide. Finally, Sira spoke up in a cold voice.

'We have plenty of eunuchs,' she said, 'but we have very few camel tenders, and the ones we do have are worthless. Even today, I need to visit my mother who is sick with a fever, and there is no slave I trust to lead my camel. Ask this slave if he knows how to lead a camel.'
My master immediately asked me, 'What do you know about camels?'

Trying hard to hide my desperate eagerness, I answered, 'I can make them kneel. I can load them with cargo. I can lead them on long trips without letting them get tired. If necessary, I can even fix their saddles and gear.'
'The slave speaks confidently enough,' my master observed. 'If you want, Sira, you can take this man to be your camel tender.'

So, I was handed over to Sira. That very day, I led her camel on a long journey to visit her sick mother. I took the opportunity to thank her for saving me. I also told her that I wasn't born a slave, but that I was the son of a free man, a respected saddle maker in Babylon. I explained a lot of my story to her. Her response really caught me off guard, and I thought about her words for a long time afterward.

'How can you call yourself a free man when your own weakness brought you to this point?' she asked. 'If a man has the soul of a slave inside him, won't he eventually become a slave no matter how he was born, just as water always seeks its own level? And if a man has the soul of a free man inside him, won't he eventually become respected and honored in his own city, no matter what bad luck he faces?'

For over a year, I was a slave and lived among the slaves, but I couldn't bring myself to act like one of them. One day, Sira asked me, 'In the evening, when the other slaves socialize and enjoy each other's company, why do you always sit alone in your tent?'
I answered her, 'I am still thinking about what you told me. I am wondering if I truly have the soul of a slave. Because I feel different from them, I can't join them, so I have to sit by myself.'

'I have to sit by myself, too,' she confessed. 'My dowry was very large, and my husband only married me because of the money. He doesn't actually want me. But what every woman truly wants is to be desired. Because he doesn't want me, and because I haven't been able to have any children, I must sit alone. If I were a man, I would rather die than be a slave like this. But the traditions of our tribe force women into slavery.'
'What do you think of me by now?' I suddenly asked her. 'Do I have the soul of a man, or do I have the soul of a slave?'
'Do you have a desire to repay the fair debts you owe in Babylon?' she parried.
'Yes, I have the desire, but I see no way.'

'If you contentedly let the years slip by and make no effort to repay, then you have nothing but the shameful soul of a slave. No man is anything else if he cannot respect himself, and no man can respect himself if he does not repay his honest debts.'
'But what can I do when I am just a slave in Syria?'
'Then stay a slave in Syria, you weakling.'
'I am not a weakling,' I denied hotly.
'Then prove it.'
'How?'

'Doesn't your great king fight his enemies in every way he can and with every force he has? Your debts are your enemies. They ran you out of Babylon. You left them alone, and they grew too strong for you. If you had fought them like a man, you could have conquered them and been honored among the townspeople. But you did not have the soul to fight them. Look how your pride has gone down, until you are now a slave in Syria.'

I thought a lot about her unkind accusations. I prepared many defensive phrases to prove that I was not a slave at heart. However, I never got the chance to use them. Three days later, Sira's maid took me to see her mistress.

'My mother is very sick again,' she said. 'Saddle the two best camels in my husband's herd. Tie on water skins and saddlebags for a long journey. The maid will give you food at the kitchen tent.' I packed the camels, wondering a great deal about the large amount of provisions the maid provided. After all, her mother lived less than a day's journey away. The maid rode the rear camel that followed us, and I led the camel of my mistress. It was just getting dark when we reached her mother's house. Sira dismissed the maid and said to me:
'Dabasir, do you have the soul of a free man or the soul of a slave?'
'The soul of a free man,' I insisted.

'Now is your chance to prove it. Your master has been drinking heavily, and his chiefs are in a stupor. Take these camels and make your escape. Here in this bag are some of your master's clothes to disguise yourself. I will say that you stole the camels and ran away while I was visiting my sick mother.'
'You have the soul of a queen,' I told her. 'I wish very much that I could lead you to happiness.'

'Happiness does not await a runaway wife who seeks it in far lands among strange people,' she responded. 'Go your own way. May the gods of the desert protect you, because the journey is long and completely barren of food or water.'

I needed no further urging. I thanked her warmly and hurried away into the night. I did not know this strange country, and I only had a dim idea of the direction toward Babylon. Still, I struck out bravely across the desert toward the hills. I rode one camel and led the other. I traveled all that night and all the next day. I was urged on by the knowledge of the terrible fate that awaited slaves who stole their master's property and tried to escape.

Late that afternoon, I reached a rough country that was just as uninhabitable as the desert. The sharp rocks bruised the feet of my faithful camels, and soon they were picking their way slowly and painfully. I met neither man nor beast along the way, and I could well understand why they avoided this inhospitable land.

From then on, it was a journey that very few men ever live to talk about. We plodded along day after day. Our food and water ran out. The heat of the sun was merciless. At the end of the ninth day, I slid from the back of my camel. I felt that I was too weak to ever remount, and I was certain I would die lost in this abandoned country.
I stretched out upon the ground and slept. I did not wake up until the very first gleam of daylight.

I sat up and looked around. There was a refreshing coolness in the morning air. My camels lay dejected not far away. All around me was a vast wasteland of broken country covered with rock, sand, and thorny bushes. There was no sign of water, and absolutely nothing for a man or a camel to eat.

Could it be that I was facing my end in this peaceful quiet? My mind was clearer than it had ever been before. My body now seemed to be of very little importance. My parched and bleeding lips, my dry and swollen tongue, and my empty stomach had all lost their supreme agonies from the day before.

I looked across into the uninviting distance, and once again the question came to me. 'Do I have the soul of a slave or the soul of a free man?' Then, with perfect clarity, I realized something. If I had the soul of a slave, I should just give up, lie down in the desert, and die. That would be a fitting end for a runaway slave.

But if I had the soul of a free man, what then? Surely I would force my way back to Babylon. I would repay the people who had trusted me. I would bring happiness to my wife who truly loved me, and I would bring peace and contentment to my parents.
'Your debts are your enemies who have run you out of Babylon,' Sira had said. Yes, it was true. Why had I refused to stand my ground like a man? Why had I allowed my wife to go back to her father?

Then a strange thing happened. The whole world seemed to be a different color. It felt as though I had been looking at it through a colored stone that was suddenly taken away. At last, I saw the true values in life.

Die in the desert? Not me! With a new vision, I saw exactly what I needed to do. First, I would go back to Babylon and face every man to whom I owed an unpaid debt. I would tell them that after years of wandering and misfortune, I had come back to pay my debts as fast as the gods would allow. Next, I would make a home for my wife, and I would become a citizen that my parents could be proud of.
My debts were my enemies, but the men I owed were my friends. They were my friends because they had trusted me and believed in me.

I staggered weakly to my feet. What did hunger matter? What did thirst matter? They were just minor incidents on the road to Babylon. The soul of a free man surged within me. I was going back to conquer my enemies and reward my friends. I thrilled with this great resolve.

The glazed eyes of my camels brightened at the new tone in my husky voice. After many attempts and with great effort, they finally got to their feet. With pitiful perseverance, they pushed on toward the north. Something deep inside me said that we would find Babylon there.

We found water. We passed into a more fertile country filled with grass and fruit. We eventually found the trail to Babylon. We found it because the soul of a free man looks at life as a series of problems to be solved, and then solves them. Meanwhile, the soul of a slave simply whines, 'What can I do when I am just a slave?'

How about you, Tarkad? Does your empty stomach make your head exceptionally clear? Are you ready to take the road that leads back to self-respect? Can you see the world in its true colors? Do you have the desire to pay your honest debts, no matter how many there may be? Do you want to once again be a man respected in Babylon?
Moisture came to the eyes of the youth. He rose eagerly to his knees.
You have shown me a vision. I already feel the soul of a free man surging within me.
But how did things go for you upon your return?
questioned an interested listener.
Where the determination is, the way can be found,
Dabasir replied.

I now had the determination, so I set out to find a way. First, I visited every single man to whom I was in debt. I begged for their patience until I could earn the money to repay them. Most of them met me gladly. A few of them insulted me, but others actually offered to help me. One man in particular gave me the exact help I needed. It was Mathon, the gold lender. He learned that I had been a camel tender in Syria. So, he sent me to old Nebatur, the camel trader. Nebatur had just been commissioned by our good king to purchase many herds of healthy camels for a great expedition. I put my knowledge of camels to very good use working with him. Gradually, I was able to repay every copper and every piece of silver. Then, at last, I could hold my head high. I finally felt that I was an honorable man among men.
Again, Dabasir turned back to his food.
Kauskor, you snail!
he called loudly to be heard in the kitchen.
The food is cold. Bring me more meat fresh from the roasting. Bring a very large portion for Tarkad, too. He is the son of my old friend, he is hungry, and he will eat with me.

So ended the tale of Dabasir, the camel trader of old Babylon. He found his own soul when he realized a great truth. It was a truth that had been known and used by wise men long before his time.

This truth has led men of all ages out of difficulties and into success. It will continue to do so for anyone who has the wisdom to understand its magic power. It is here for any man to use who reads these lines.

**WHERE THE DETERMINATION IS,**
**THE WAY CAN BE FOUND**


The Clay Tablets From Babylon
St. Swithin's College

Nottingham University

Newark-on-Trent

Nottingham

Professor Franklin Caldwell,

Care of British Scientific Expedition,

Hillah, Mesopotamia.

October 21, 1934,

My dear Professor: The five clay tablets from your recent excavation in the ruins of Babylon arrived on the same boat as your letter. I have been completely fascinated by them. I have spent many pleasant hours translating their inscriptions. I should have answered your letter immediately, but I delayed until I could finish the attached translations.

The tablets arrived without any damage, thanks to your careful use of preservatives and excellent packing.

You will be just as astonished by the story they tell as we were in the laboratory. We usually expect the dim and distant past to speak of romance and adventure. You know, "Arabian Nights" types of stories. But instead, these tablets reveal the struggles of a person named Dabasir trying to pay off his debts. It makes you realize that conditions in this old world have not changed nearly as much in five thousand years as you might expect.

It is strange, but these ancient inscriptions really shook me up, as my students might say. As a college professor, I am supposed to be a thinking human being with a working knowledge of most subjects. Yet, here comes this old fellow straight out of the dust-covered ruins of Babylon. He offers a way to pay off my debts and acquire jingling gold in my wallet that I had never even heard of.

It is a pleasant thought, and it will be very interesting to see if his method works just as well today as it did in old Babylon. Mrs. Shrewsbury and I are planning to try his plan on our own finances, which could certainly use the improvement.

I wish you the best of luck in your worthy expedition. I am eagerly waiting for another opportunity to assist you, and I remain

Yours sincerely,

Alfred H. Shrewsbury,
Department of Archaeology.

Tablet No. I

Now, as the moon becomes full, I, Dabasir, have just returned from slavery in Syria. I am determined to pay off my many fair debts. I want to become a wealthy man worthy of respect in my native city of Babylon. So, I am carving a permanent record of my affairs into this clay. This record will guide and help me achieve my high ambitions.

Under the wise advice of my good friend Mathon, the gold lender, I am determined to follow an exact plan. He says this plan will lead any honorable man out of debt and into wealth and self-respect.

This plan includes three purposes, which represent my hopes and desires.

First, the plan provides for my future prosperity.

**Therefore, one-tenth of everything I earn will be set aside as my own to keep.** Mathon spoke wisely when he said:
A man who keeps gold and silver in his purse that he does not need to spend is good to his family and loyal to his king.
A man who has only a few coppers in his purse is indifferent to his family and indifferent to his king.
But a man who has nothing in his purse is unkind to his family and disloyal to his king, because his own heart is bitter.
Therefore, the man who wishes to achieve success must have coin that he can keep to jingle in his purse. This way, he will have love for his family and loyalty to his king in his heart.

Second, the plan ensures that I will support and clothe my good wife. She has loyally returned to me from her father's house. Mathon says that taking good care of a faithful wife puts self-respect into a man's heart. It also adds strength and determination to his goals.

**Therefore, seven-tenths of all I earn will be used to provide a home, clothes to wear, and food to eat.** It will also include a little extra to spend, so our lives will not lack pleasure and enjoyment. However, he strongly warned me to be extremely careful. We must not spend more than seven-tenths of what I earn for these worthy purposes. The success of the plan lies right here.

I must live entirely on this portion. I must never use more. I must never buy what I cannot pay for out of this share.

Tablet No. II

Third, the plan ensures that my debts will be paid out of my earnings.

**Therefore, every time the moon is full, two-tenths of all I have earned will be divided honorably and fairly.** I will distribute this among those who trusted me and to whom I owe money. This way, all my debts will surely be paid in due time. So, I am engraving here the name of every man I am indebted to, along with the honest amount of my debt.

Fahru, the cloth weaver, 2 silver, 6 copper.

Sinjar, the couch maker, 1 silver.

Ahmar, my friend, 3 silver, 1 copper.

Zankar, my friend, 4 silver, 7 copper.


Askamir, my friend, 1 silver, 3 copper.

Harinsir, the Jewelmaker, 6 silver, 2 copper.

Diarbeker, my father's friend, 4 silver, 1 copper.

Alkahad, the house owner, 14 silver.

Mathon, the gold lender, 9 silver.

Birejik, the farmer, 1 silver, 7 copper.

(From here on, disintegrated. Cannot be deciphered.)

Tablet No. III

I owe these creditors a total of 119 pieces of silver and 141 pieces of copper. I owed these sums and saw no way to repay them. In my foolishness, I allowed my wife to return to her father. I left my native city to seek easy wealth elsewhere. Instead, I only found disaster and was sold into the degradation of slavery.

Now Mathon has shown me how to repay my debts in small portions of my earnings. I finally realize the great extent of my foolishness in running away from the results of my extravagant spending. Therefore, I visited my creditors. I explained to them that I have no resources to pay them, except for my ability to earn. I intend to apply two-tenths of all I earn toward my debts, dividing it evenly and honestly. This is how much I can pay, and no more. If they are patient, my obligations will eventually be paid in full.

Ahmar, whom I considered my best friend, insulted me bitterly. I left him in humiliation. Birejik, the farmer, pleaded for me to pay him first because he badly needed the help. Alkahad, the house owner, was very disagreeable. He insisted that he would cause trouble for me unless I settled my debt in full soon.

All the rest willingly accepted my proposal. Because of this, I am more determined than ever to carry it through. I am convinced that it is easier to pay one's just debts than to avoid them. Even though I cannot meet the needs and demands of a few of my creditors, I will deal fairly and impartially with everyone.

Tablet No. IV

The moon is full again. I have worked hard with a clear mind. My good wife has supported my intention to pay my creditors. Because of our wise determination, I earned nineteen pieces of silver over the past month. I made this money by buying camels with strong lungs and good legs for Nebatur.

I divided this money according to the plan. I set aside one-tenth to keep for myself. I gave seven-tenths to my good wife to pay for our living expenses. The remaining two-tenths, I divided among my creditors as evenly as possible using copper coins.

I did not see Ahmar, but I left his payment with his wife. Birejik was so pleased that he tried to kiss my hand. Old Alkahad was the only one who grumbled. He said I must pay faster. I replied that if I was allowed to be well-fed and free of worry, that alone would help me pay him faster. All the others thanked me and praised my efforts.

Therefore, at the end of one month, my debt is reduced by almost four pieces of silver. I also possess almost two pieces of silver of my own, which no man can claim. My heart is lighter than it has been in a very long time.

The moon is full again. I worked hard, but with poor success. I was able to buy only a few camels. I earned just eleven pieces of silver. Nevertheless, my good wife and I stood by our plan. We bought no new clothing, and we ate little besides herbs.

Once again, I paid ourselves one-tenth of the eleven pieces of silver, and we lived on seven-tenths. I was surprised when Ahmar praised my payment, even though it was small. Birejik did the same. Alkahad flew into a rage. However, when I told him to give back his portion if he did not want it, he accepted it. The others were content, just as before.

The moon is full again, and I am filled with joy. I intercepted a fine herd of camels and bought many healthy ones. Because of this, my earnings were forty-two pieces of silver. This month, my wife and I bought much-needed sandals and clothing. We also dined well on meat and fowl.

We paid more than eight pieces of silver to our creditors. Even Alkahad did not protest.

The plan is truly great. It leads us out of debt and gives us wealth that is ours to keep.

The moon has been full three times since I last carved upon this clay. Each month, I paid myself one-tenth of all I earned. My good wife and I lived on seven-tenths, even though it was difficult at times. And each month, I paid two-tenths to my creditors.

I now have twenty-one pieces of silver of my own in my purse. This makes me stand tall and feel proud to walk among my friends. My wife keeps our home beautifully and is dressed elegantly. We are very happy living together.

The plan is incredibly valuable. Has it not turned an ex-slave into an honorable man?

Tablet No. V

The moon is full once again. I remember that it has been a long time since I last carved upon the clay. In truth, twelve months have come and gone. But I will not neglect my record today, because today I paid the last of my debts. My good wife and my thankful self will celebrate with a great feast today. We have finally achieved our goal.

Many things happened during my final visit to my creditors that I will remember for a long time. Ahmar begged for my forgiveness for his unkind words. He told me that out of everyone, I was the one he most wanted as a friend.

Old Alkahad is not so bad after all. He said to me,

You were once a piece of soft clay, easily pressed and molded by any hand that touched you. But now you are a piece of bronze, capable of holding a sharp edge. If you ever need silver or gold, come to me.

He is not the only one who holds me in high regard. Many others speak to me with deep respect. My good wife looks at me with a light in her eyes that gives a man true confidence in himself.

Yet, it is the plan that created my success. It enabled me to pay all my debts and to jingle both gold and silver in my purse. I highly recommend it to anyone who wishes to get ahead. Truly, if this plan can enable an ex-slave to pay his debts and have gold in his purse, won't it help any man find independence? I am not finished with the plan myself. I am convinced that if I continue to follow it, it will make me a very rich man.

St. Swithin's College
Nottingham University
Newark-on-Trent
Nottingham

Professor Franklin Caldwell,
Care of British Scientific Expedition,
Hillah, Mesopotamia.


My dear professor:

November 7th, 1936.

If, in your further digging into those ruins of Babylon, you encounter the ghost of a former resident, an old camel trader named Dabasir, do me a favor. Tell him that his scribbling upon those clay tablets, so long ago, has earned for him the lifelong gratitude of a couple of college folks back here in England.

You will possibly remember my writing a year ago that Mrs. Shrewsbury and I intended to try his plan for getting out of debt and at the same time having gold to jingle. You may have guessed, even though we tried to keep it from our friends, our desperate straits.

We were frightfully humiliated for years by a lot of old debts and worried sick for fear some of the tradespeople might start a scandal that would force me out of the college. We paid and paid—every shilling we could squeeze out of income—but it was hardly enough to hold things even. Besides, we were forced to do all our buying where we could get further credit, regardless of higher costs.

It developed into one of those vicious circles that grow worse instead of better. Our struggles were getting hopeless. We could not move to less costly rooms because we owed the landlord. There did not appear to be anything we could do to improve our situation.

Then, here comes your acquaintance, the old camel trader from Babylon, with a plan to do just what we wished to accomplish. He really stirred us up to follow his system. We made a list of all our debts, and I took it around and showed it to everyone we owed.

I explained how it was simply impossible for me to ever pay them the way things were going along. They could readily see this themselves from the figures. Then I explained that the only way I saw to pay in full was to set aside twenty percent of my income each month to be divided proportionally. This would pay them in full in a little over two years. I added that, in the meantime, we would go on a cash basis and give them the further benefit of our cash purchases.

They were really quite decent. Our greengrocer, a wise old man, put it in a way that helped to bring around the rest.
If you pay for all you buy and then pay some on what you owe, that is better than you have done, for you haven't paid down the account at all in three years.

Finally, I secured all their names to an agreement binding them not to bother us as long as the twenty percent of our income was paid regularly. Then we began scheming on how to live upon seventy percent. We were determined to keep that extra ten percent to jingle. The thought of silver and possibly gold was most alluring.

It was like having an adventure to make the change. We enjoyed figuring this way and that, to live comfortably upon that remaining seventy percent. We started with rent and managed to secure a fair reduction. Next, we put our favorite brands of tea and such under suspicion and were agreeably surprised how often we could purchase superior qualities at less cost.

It is too long a story for a letter, but anyhow it did not prove difficult. We managed, and right cheerfully at that. What a relief it proved to have our affairs in such a shape that we were no longer persecuted by past due accounts.

I must not neglect, however, to tell you about that extra ten percent we were supposed to jingle. Well, we did jingle it for some time. Now don't laugh too soon. You see, that is the sporty part. It is the real fun, to start accumulating money that you do not want to spend. There is more pleasure in running up such a surplus than there could be in spending it.

After we had jingled to our hearts' content, we found a more profitable use for it. We took up an investment upon which we could pay that ten percent each month. This is proving to be the most satisfying part of our regeneration. It is the first thing we pay out of my check.

There is a most gratifying sense of security to know our investment is growing steadily. By the time my teaching days are over, it should be a snug sum, large enough so the income will take care of us from then on.

All this out of my same old check. It is difficult to believe, yet absolutely true. All our debts are being gradually paid, and at the same time our investment is increasing. Besides, we get along financially even better than before. Who would believe there could be such a difference in results between following a financial plan and just drifting along?

At the end of the next year, when all our old bills will have been paid, we will have more to pay upon our investment, besides some extra for travel.

We are determined never again to permit our living expenses to exceed seventy percent of our income. Now you can understand why we would like to extend our personal thanks to that old man whose plan saved us from our "Hell on Earth."

He knew. He had been through it all. He wanted others to benefit from his own bitter experiences. That is why he spent tedious hours carving his message upon the clay. He had a real message for fellow sufferers. It is a message so important that after five thousand years it has risen out of the ruins of Babylon, just as true and just as vital as the day it was buried.

Yours sincerely,

Alfred H. Shrewsbury,
Department of Archaeology.

The Luckiest Man in Babylon

At the head of his caravan proudly rode Sharru Nada, the merchant prince of Babylon. He liked fine cloth and wore rich and becoming robes. He liked fine animals and sat easily upon his spirited Arabian stallion. To look at him, one would hardly have guessed his advanced years. Certainly, they would not have suspected that he was inwardly troubled.

The journey from Damascus is long and the hardships of the desert are many. He did not mind these. The Arab tribes are fierce and eager to loot rich caravans. He did not fear these, for his many fleet-mounted guards were a safe protection.

He was disturbed about the youth at his side, whom he was bringing from Damascus. This was Hadan Gula, the grandson of his partner from other years, Arad Gula. He felt he owed Arad Gula a debt of gratitude that could never be repaid. He would like to do something for this grandson. But the more he considered this, the more difficult it seemed because of the youth himself.

Eyeing the young man's rings and earrings, he thought to himself,

He thinks jewels are for men, yet he has his grandfather's strong face. But his grandfather wore no such gaudy robes. Still, I asked him to come, hoping I might help him get a start for himself and get away from the wreck his father has made of their inheritance.
Hadan Gula broke in upon his thoughts.
Why do you work so hard, always riding with your caravan upon its long journeys? Do you never take time to enjoy life?
Sharru Nada smiled.
To enjoy life?
he repeated.
What would you do to enjoy life if you were Sharru Nada?

If I had wealth equal to yours, I would live like a prince. I would never ride across the hot desert. I would spend the shekels as fast as they came to my purse. I would wear the richest of robes and the rarest of jewels. That would be a life to my liking, a life worth living.
Both men laughed.
Your grandfather wore no jewels.
Sharru Nada spoke before he thought, then continued jokingly,
Would you leave no time for work?
Work was made for slaves,

Hadan Gula responded.

Sharru Nada bit his lip but made no reply. They rode in silence until the trail led them to the slope. Here he reined his mount and pointed to the green valley far away.

See, there is the valley. Look far down and you can faintly see the walls of Babylon. The tower is the Temple of Bel. If your eyes are sharp, you might even see the smoke from the eternal fire upon its crest.
So that is Babylon? I have always longed to see the wealthiest city in all the world,
Hadan Gula commented.
Babylon, where my grandfather started his fortune. I wish he were still alive. We would not be so sorely pressed.
Why wish for his spirit to linger on earth beyond its allotted time? You and your father can carry on his good work.
Sadly, neither of us has his gift. My father and I do not know his secret for attracting golden shekels.

Sharru Nada did not reply. He gave rein to his mount and rode thoughtfully down the trail to the valley. The caravan followed behind them in a cloud of reddish dust. Some time later, they reached the Kings' highway and turned south through the irrigated farms.

Three old men plowing a field caught Sharru Nada's attention. They seemed strangely familiar. How ridiculous! You do not pass a field after forty years and find the same men plowing there. Yet, something within him said they were the exact same men. One held the plow with an uncertain grip. The others plodded laboriously beside the oxen. They were ineffectually beating the oxen with their barrel staves to keep them pulling.

Forty years ago, he had envied these men! He would have gladly exchanged places with them! But what a difference he saw now. He looked back with pride at his trailing caravan. He saw well-chosen camels and donkeys, loaded high with valuable goods from Damascus. All of this was just one of his many possessions.

He pointed to the plowers.
They are still plowing the same field where they were forty years ago,
he said.
They certainly look like it, but why do you think they are the same men?
I saw them there,

Sharru Nada replied. Memories were racing rapidly through his mind. Why couldn't he just bury the past and live in the present? Then he saw the smiling face of Arad Gula, as clear as a picture. The barrier between himself and the cynical youth beside him completely dissolved.

But how could he help such an arrogant youth? The young man had spendthrift ideas and bejeweled hands. Sharru Nada could offer plenty of work to willing workers. But he had nothing for men who considered themselves too good for work. Yet, he owed it to Arad Gula to do something. It could not be a half-hearted attempt. He and Arad Gula had never done things that way. They were not that sort of men.

A plan came to him almost in a flash. There were objections. He had to consider his own family and his own social standing. It would be cruel, and it would hurt. But being a man of quick decisions, he waved away his objections and decided to act.
Would you be interested in hearing how your worthy grandfather and I joined in the partnership that proved so profitable?
he asked.
Why not just tell me how you made your golden shekels? That is all I really need to know,

the young man parried.

Sharru Nada ignored the reply and continued.

We start with those men plowing. I was no older than you are now. I was marching in a column of men. As we approached, good old Megiddo, a farmer, scoffed at the sloppy way they plowed. Megiddo was chained right next to me. 'Look at those lazy fellows,' he protested. 'The plow holder makes no effort to plow deep. The beaters do not even keep the oxen in the furrow. How can they expect to raise a good crop with such poor plowing?'
Did you say Megiddo was chained to you?
Hadan Gula asked in surprise.

Yes, we had bronze collars around our necks. There was a length of heavy chain between us. Next to him was Zabado, the sheep thief. I had known him back in Harroun. At the end of the line was a man we called Pirate, because he would not tell us his real name. We judged him to be a sailor. He had entwined serpents tattooed on his chest in a sailor's fashion. The column was made up like this so the men could walk in rows of four.
You were chained as a slave?
Hadan Gula asked incredulously.
Did your grandfather not tell you I was once a slave?
He often spoke of you, but he never hinted at this.
He was a man you could trust with your innermost secrets. You, too, are a man I can trust, am I right?
Sharru Nada looked him squarely in the eye.
You may rely on my silence, but I am simply amazed. Tell me, how did you come to be a slave?
Sharru Nada shrugged his shoulders.

Any man may find himself a slave. It was a gaming house and barley beer that brought me disaster. I was the victim of my brother's foolishness. In a brawl, he killed his friend. I was bonded to the widow by my father. He was desperate to keep my brother from being prosecuted under the law. When my father could not raise the silver to free me, the widow sold me to the slave dealer in anger.
What a shame and an injustice!
Hadan Gula protested.
But tell me, how did you regain your freedom?

We will come to that, but not yet. Let us continue my tale. As we passed, the plowers jeered at us. One took off his ragged hat and bowed low. He called out, 'Welcome to Babylon, guests of the King! He waits for you on the city walls where the banquet is spread. Enjoy your mud bricks and onion soup!' With that, they all laughed uproariously.
Pirate flew into a rage and cursed at them roundly. 'What do those men mean about the King awaiting us on the walls?' I asked him.
'You march to the city walls to carry bricks until your back breaks,' he said. 'Maybe they will beat you to death before it even breaks. But they won't beat me. I will kill them.'
Then Megiddo spoke up. 'It doesn't make sense to me to talk of masters beating willing, hard-working slaves to death,' he said. 'Masters like good slaves. They treat them well.'
'Who wants to work hard?' Zabado commented. 'Those plowers are wise fellows. They are not breaking their backs. They are just pretending to work.'

'You cannot get ahead by shirking your duties,' Megiddo protested. 'If you plow a hectare, that is a good day's work. Any master knows it. But when you only plow a half, that is shirking. I do not shirk. I like to work, and I like to do good work. Work is the best friend I have ever known. It has brought me all the good things I have ever had. It gave me my farm, my cows, my crops, everything.'

'Yeah, and where are all those things now?' scoffed Zabado. 'I figure it pays better to be smart and get by without working. You just watch me. If we are sold to the walls, I will be carrying the water bag or doing some easy job. Meanwhile, you, who loves to work, will be breaking your back carrying bricks.' He laughed his silly laugh.

Terror gripped me that night. I could not sleep. I crowded close to the guard rope. When the others slept, I caught the attention of Godoso. He was doing the first guard watch. He was one of those brigand Arabs. He was the sort of rogue who, if he robbed you of your purse, would think he also had to cut your throat.
'Tell me, Godoso,' I whispered. 'When we get to Babylon, will we be sold to the walls?'
'Why do you want to know?' he questioned cautiously.
'Can you not understand?' I pleaded. 'I am young, and I want to live. I do not want to be worked or beaten to death on the walls. Is there any chance for me to get a good master?'

He whispered back. 'I will tell you something. You are a good fellow. You gave me no trouble. Most times we go first to the slave market. Listen now. When buyers come, tell them you are a good worker. Tell them you like to work hard for a good master. Make them want to buy you. If you do not make them buy you, the next day you will carry bricks. It is mighty hard work.'

After he walked away, I lay in the warm sand. I looked up at the stars and thought about work. Megiddo had said that work was his best friend. This made me wonder if work could be my best friend, too. It certainly would be if it helped me get out of this situation.

When Megiddo awoke, I whispered my good news to him. It was our one ray of hope as we marched toward Babylon. Late in the afternoon, we approached the walls. We could see the lines of men, looking just like black ants. They were climbing up and down the steep diagonal paths. As we drew closer, we were amazed at the thousands of men working. Some were digging in the moat. Others mixed the dirt into mud bricks. The greatest number of men were carrying the bricks in large baskets. They carried them up those steep trails to the masons.*

Overseers cursed the slow workers. They cracked bullwhips over the backs of those who failed to keep in line. Poor, worn-out fellows staggered and fell beneath their heavy baskets. They were unable to rise again. If the lash failed to bring them to their feet, they were pushed to the side of the paths. They were left writhing in agony. Soon they would be dragged down to join other broken bodies beside the roadway, awaiting unmarked graves. As I beheld the ghastly sight, I shuddered. So this was what awaited my father's son if he failed at the slave market.

*The famous works of ancient Babylon—its walls, temples, hanging gardens, and great canals—were built by slave labor. These slaves were mainly prisoners of war, which explains the inhuman treatment they received. This force of workmen also included many citizens of Babylon and its provinces. They had been sold into slavery because of crimes or financial troubles. It was a common custom for men to put themselves, their wives, or their children up as a bond. This guaranteed the payment of loans, legal judgments, or other obligations. In case of default, those who were bonded were sold into slavery.

Godoso had been right. We were taken through the gates of the city to the slave prison. The next morning, we were marched to the pens in the market. Here, the rest of the men huddled in fear. Only the whips of our guards could keep them moving so the buyers could examine them. Megiddo and I eagerly talked to every man who permitted us to speak to him.
The slave dealer brought soldiers from the King's Guard. They shackled Pirate and brutally beat him when he protested. As they led him away, I actually felt sorry for him.

Megiddo felt that we would soon be separated. When no buyers were near, he spoke to me earnestly. He wanted to impress upon me how valuable work would be to my future. 'Some men hate work,' he told me. 'They make it their enemy. It is better to treat it like a friend. Make yourself like it. Do not mind if it is hard. If you think about the beautiful house you are building, who cares if the beams are heavy? Who cares if it is a long walk to the well to carry water for the plaster? Promise me, boy. If you get a master, work for him as hard as you can. If he does not appreciate all that you do, never mind. Remember that work well done does good for the man who does it. It makes him a better man.' He stopped speaking as a burly farmer came to the enclosure and looked at us critically.

Megiddo asked about his farm and his crops. He soon convinced the farmer that he would be a valuable worker. After violent bargaining with the slave dealer, the farmer drew a fat purse from beneath his robe. Soon, Megiddo had followed his new master out of sight.

A few other men were sold during the morning. At noon, Godoso confided in me. He said the dealer was frustrated and would not stay another night. Instead, he would take everyone who remained at sundown to the King's buyer. I was becoming desperate. Just then, a fat, good-natured man walked up to the wall and asked if there was a baker among us.

I approached him and said, 'Why should a good baker like yourself seek out another baker with inferior skills? Would it not be easier to teach a willing man like myself your expert ways? Look at me. I am young, strong, and I like to work. Give me a chance, and I will do my absolute best to earn gold and silver for your purse.'

He was impressed by my willingness. He began bargaining with the dealer. The dealer had never noticed me since he bought me, but now he spoke enthusiastically about my abilities, my good health, and my positive attitude. I felt like a fat ox being sold to a butcher. At last, much to my joy, the deal was closed. I followed my new master away, thinking I was the luckiest man in Babylon.

My new home was much to my liking. My master, Nana-naid, taught me how to grind the barley in the stone bowl that stood in the courtyard. He showed me how to build the fire in the oven. He then taught me how to grind the sesame flour very fine for the honey cakes. I had a couch in the shed where his grain was stored. The old slave housekeeper, Swasti, fed me well. She was very pleased with the way I helped her with the heavy tasks.
Here was the chance I had longed for. I could finally make myself valuable to my master and, I hoped, find a way to earn my freedom.

I asked Nana-naid to show me how to knead the bread and bake it. He did this, and he was very pleased with my willingness to learn. Later, when I could do this well, I asked him to teach me how to make the honey cakes. Soon, I was doing all the baking. My master was glad to be idle. However, Swasti shook her head in disapproval. 'Having no work to do is bad for any man,' she declared.

I felt it was time for me to think of a way to earn coins to buy my freedom. Since the baking was finished by noon, I thought Nana-naid might approve if I found profitable work for the afternoons. He might even share my earnings with me. Then, a thought came to me. Why not bake extra honey cakes and sell them to hungry men on the streets of the city?

I presented my plan to Nana-naid. I asked him, 'If I can use my afternoons, after the baking is finished, to earn coins for you, would it not be fair for you to share those earnings with me? That way, I could have money of my own to spend on the things every man desires and needs.'

'Fair enough, fair enough,' he admitted. When I told him of my plan to sell our honey cakes, he was very pleased. 'Here is what we will do,' he suggested. 'You will sell them at two for a penny. Half of the pennies will be mine to pay for the flour, the honey, and the wood used to bake them. Of the remaining pennies, I will take half, and you will keep half.'

I was very pleased by his generous offer. I was allowed to keep one-fourth of my total sales for myself! That night, I worked late to build a tray to display the cakes. Nana-naid gave me one of his worn robes so that I might look presentable. Swasti helped me patch it up and wash it clean.

The next day, I baked an extra supply of honey cakes. They looked brown and tempting on the tray as I walked down the street, loudly calling out my wares. At first, no one seemed interested, and I became discouraged. I kept going, though. Later in the afternoon, as men became hungry, the cakes began to sell. Soon, my tray was completely empty.

Nana-naid was very pleased with my success and gladly paid me my share. I was delighted to own pennies of my own. Megiddo had been right when he said a master appreciated good work from his slaves. That night, I was so excited over my success that I could hardly sleep. I tried to calculate how much I could earn in a single year, and how many years it would take to buy my freedom.

As I went out with my tray of cakes every day, I soon found regular customers. One of these men was none other than your grandfather, Arad Gula. He was a rug merchant who sold his goods to housewives. He traveled from one end of the city to the other. He was always accompanied by a donkey loaded high with rugs, along with a black slave to tend to the animal. He would buy two cakes for himself and two for his slave. He always lingered to talk with me while they ate.

Your grandfather said something to me one day that I will always remember. He told me, 'I like your cakes, boy. But better still, I like the fine sense of enterprise with which you offer them. That kind of spirit can carry you far on the road to success.'

But how can you understand, Hadan Gula, what such words of encouragement meant to a slave boy? I was lonesome in a great city. I was struggling with everything I had to find a way out of my humiliation.

As the months went by, I continued to add pennies to my purse. It began to have a comforting weight upon my belt. Work was proving to be my best friend, just as Megiddo had said. I was happy, but Swasti was worried.
'I am afraid for your master,' she protested. 'He spends far too much time at the gaming houses.'

One day, I was overjoyed to meet my friend Megiddo on the street. He was leading three donkeys loaded with vegetables to the market. 'I am doing incredibly well,' he told me. 'My master truly appreciates my hard work. I am now a foreman! You see, he trusts the marketing to me, and he is even sending for my family. Work is helping me recover from my great troubles. Someday, it will help me buy my freedom so I can once more own a farm of my own.'

Time went on, and Nana-naid became more and more anxious for me to return from selling. He would be waiting the moment I returned. He would eagerly count and divide our money. He also constantly urged me to seek out new markets and increase my sales.

Often, I went outside the city gates to sell to the overseers of the slaves who were building the walls. I hated returning to those disagreeable sights. However, I found that the overseers were very generous buyers. One day, I was surprised to see Zabado waiting in line to fill his basket with bricks. He was gaunt and bent over. His back was covered in welts and sores from the overseers' whips. I felt sorry for him. I handed him a cake, and he crushed it into his mouth like a hungry animal. Seeing the greedy, desperate look in his eyes, I ran away before he could grab my tray.

'Why do you work so hard?' Arad Gula asked me one day. It was almost the exact same question you asked me today, do you remember? I told him what Megiddo had said about work, and how it was proving to be my best friend. I proudly showed him my wallet full of pennies. I explained how I was saving them to buy my freedom.
'When you are free, what will you do?' he inquired.
'Then,' I answered, 'I intend to become a merchant.'
At that moment, he confided in me. He revealed something I had never suspected. 'You do not know this,' he said, 'but I, too, am a slave. I am in a partnership with my master.'
Stop!
demanded Hadan Gula.
I will not listen to lies that defame my grandfather! He was no slave.

His eyes blazed with anger.

Sharru Nada remained calm.

I honor him for rising above his misfortune to become a leading citizen of Damascus. Are you, his grandson, cast from the same mold? Are you man enough to face the true facts? Or do you prefer to live under false illusions?
Hadan Gula straightened up in his saddle. In a voice suppressed with deep emotion, he replied.

My grandfather was beloved by everyone. His good deeds were countless. When the famine came, did his gold not buy grain in Egypt? Did his caravans not bring it to Damascus and distribute it to the people so that no one would starve? Yet now you say he was nothing but a despised slave in Babylon.
If he had remained a slave in Babylon, then he might well have been despised,
Sharru Nada replied.
But through his own efforts, he became a great man in Damascus. The Gods indeed forgave his misfortunes and honored him with their respect.
After telling me that he was a slave,
Sharru Nada continued,

he explained how anxious he had been to earn his freedom. Now that he finally had enough money to buy it, he was deeply disturbed about what he should do. He was no longer making good sales, and he was terrified to lose the support of his master.

I protested his indecision. I told him, 'Cling to your master no longer! Get the feeling of being a free man once again. Act like a free man, and succeed like one! Decide what you desire to accomplish. Then, let work aid you in achieving it!' He went on his way, saying he was glad I had shamed him for his cowardice.

One day, I went outside the gates again. I was surprised to find a massive crowd gathering there. When I asked a man for an explanation, he replied, 'Have you not heard? An escaped slave who murdered one of the King's guards has been brought to justice. He will be flogged to death today for his crime. Even the King himself is going to be here.'

The crowd around the flogging post was so dense that I was afraid to go near. I worried my tray of honey cakes would be knocked over. Therefore, I climbed up the unfinished wall so I could see over the heads of the people. I was fortunate enough to get a view of King Nebuchadnezzar himself as he rode by in his golden chariot. I had never beheld such grandeur. His robes and the hangings of his chariot were made of gorgeous gold cloth and velvet.

I could not see the actual flogging, though I could hear the shrieks of the poor slave. I wondered how someone as noble as our handsome King could endure watching such suffering. However, when I saw that he was laughing and joking with his nobles, I realized he was cruel. I suddenly understood why such inhuman tasks were demanded of the slaves building the city walls.

After the slave was dead, his body was hung upon a pole by a rope attached to his leg so that everyone could see it. As the crowd began to thin out, I went closer. On his hairy chest, I saw a tattoo of two entwined serpents. It was Pirate. The next time I met Arad Gula, he was a completely changed man. He greeted me full of enthusiasm. 'Behold!' he said. 'The slave you knew is now a free man! There was magic in your words. Already, my sales and my profits are increasing. My wife is overjoyed. She was a free woman, the niece of my former master. She strongly desires that we move to a new city where no man will know I was once a slave. That way, our children will be above reproach for their father's past misfortune. Work has truly become my best helper. It has enabled me to recapture my confidence and my skill to sell.'
I was absolutely overjoyed. I was so glad that I had been able to repay him, even in a small way, for the wonderful encouragement he had given me.

*Slave customs in ancient Babylon might seem confusing to us today, but they were strictly regulated by law. For example, a slave could own property of any kind. They could even own other slaves, and their master had no claim over them. Slaves married freely with non-slaves, and children born to free mothers were also free. Most of the city's merchants were actually slaves. Many of these enslaved merchants worked in partnership with their masters and were wealthy in their own right.*

One evening, Swasti came to me in deep distress. She said, 'Your master is in trouble, and I fear for him. Some months ago, he lost a lot of money at the gambling tables. He hasn't paid the farmer for his grain or his honey. He hasn't paid the money lender. They are all angry, and they are threatening him.'
I replied thoughtlessly,
Why should we worry about his foolishness? We aren't responsible for him.
She said,

Foolish youth, you don't understand. He gave your title to the money lender to secure a loan. Under the law, the money lender can claim you and sell you. I don't know what to do. He is a good master. Why? Oh why, should such trouble come to him?
Swasti's fears were not groundless. While I was doing the baking the next morning, the money lender returned with a man he called Sasi. This man looked me over and decided I would do.

The money lender didn't wait for my master to return. He just told Swasti to tell him that he had taken me. With only the robe on my back and the purse of pennies hanging safely from my belt, I was hurried away from the unfinished baking.

I was swept away from my dearest hopes, just like a hurricane snatches a tree from the forest and casts it into the crashing sea. Once again, a gambling house and barley beer had brought me to disaster.

Sasi was a blunt and gruff man. As he led me across the city, I told him about the good work I had been doing for Nana-naid. I said I hoped to do good work for him, too. But his reply offered no encouragement.

He told me, 'I don't like this work, and my master doesn't like it either. The King told him to send me to build a section of the Grand Canal. My master told me to buy more slaves, work them hard, and finish quickly. Bah! How can any man finish a big job quickly?'

Picture a desert without a single tree. There were just low shrubs and a sun burning with such fury that the water in our barrels became too hot to drink. Then, picture rows of men going down into a deep excavation. They lugged heavy baskets of dirt up soft, dusty trails from daylight until dark. Picture food served in open troughs, where we had to help ourselves like pigs. We had no tents, and we had no straw for beds. That was the situation I found myself in. I buried my wallet in a marked spot, wondering if I would ever dig it up again.

At first, I worked with good will. But as the months dragged on, I felt my spirit breaking. Then, the heat fever took hold of my weary body. I lost my appetite and could barely stomach the mutton and vegetables. At night, I tossed and turned in unhappy wakefulness.

In my misery, I wondered if Zabado had the best plan after all. He tried to avoid work and keep his back from being broken. But then I recalled my last sight of him, and I knew his plan was not a good one.
I thought of Pirate with his bitterness. I wondered if it might be just as well to fight and kill. But the memory of his bleeding body reminded me that his plan was also useless.
Then I remembered my last sight of Megiddo. His hands were deeply calloused from hard work, but his heart was light, and there was happiness on his face. His was the best plan.

Yet, I was just as willing to work as Megiddo. He couldn't have worked harder than me. So why didn't my work bring me happiness and success? Was it really work that brought Megiddo happiness, or were happiness and success simply left up to the gods? Was I going to work the rest of my life without getting what I wanted, without ever finding happiness or success? All of these questions were jumbled in my mind, and I didn't have an answer. I was deeply confused.

Several days later, it seemed like I was at the end of my endurance. My questions were still unanswered when Sasi sent for me. A messenger had come from my master to take me back to Babylon. I dug up my precious wallet, wrapped myself in the tattered remains of my robe, and went on my way.
As we rode, the same thoughts of a hurricane whirling me back and forth kept racing through my feverish brain. I felt like I was living the weird words of an old chant from my hometown of Harroun:

Besetting a man like a whirlwind,
Driving him like a storm,
Whose course no one can follow,
Whose destiny no one can foretell.
Was I destined to always be punished for things I didn't understand? What new miseries and disappointments were waiting for me?
When we rode into the courtyard of my master's house, imagine my surprise when I saw Arad Gula waiting for me! He helped me down and hugged me like a long-lost brother.

As we walked together, I tried to follow behind him as a slave should follow his master, but he wouldn't allow it. He put his arm around me and said, 'I hunted everywhere for you. When I had almost given up hope, I met Swasti. She told me about the money lender, who then directed me to your new owner. He drove a hard bargain and made me pay an outrageous price, but you are worth it. Your philosophy and your drive have inspired my new success.'
I interrupted,
That was Megiddo's philosophy, not mine.
He insisted,
Megiddo's and yours. Thanks to both of you, we are going to Damascus, and I need you as my partner.
Then he exclaimed,
Look! In just one moment, you will be a free man!

As he said this, he pulled out the clay tablet carrying my title from under his robe. He raised it above his head and smashed it into a hundred pieces on the cobblestones. With gleeful excitement, he stomped on the fragments until they were nothing but dust.
Tears of gratitude filled my eyes. I knew I was the luckiest man in Babylon.

You see, in the time of my greatest distress, work proved to be my best friend. My willingness to work saved me from being sold to the slave gangs on the city walls. It also impressed your grandfather so much that he chose me to be his partner.
Then Hadan Gula asked,
Was hard work my grandfather's secret key to the golden shekels?
Sharru Nada replied,
It was the only key he had when I first met him. Your grandfather enjoyed working. The gods appreciated his efforts and rewarded him generously.
Hadan Gula said thoughtfully,

I am beginning to see. Work attracted his many friends, who admired his hard work and the success it brought. Work brought him the honors he enjoyed so much in Damascus. Work brought him all those great things I admire. And I used to think work was only fit for slaves.
Sharru Nada commented,

Life is rich with many pleasures for men to enjoy. Each has its place. I am very glad that work is not just reserved for slaves. If that were the case, I would be deprived of my greatest pleasure. I enjoy many things in life, but nothing takes the place of hard work.

Sharru Nada and Hadan Gula rode in the shadows of the towering walls, straight up to the massive bronze gates of Babylon. As they approached, the gate guards jumped to attention and respectfully saluted an honored citizen. With his head held high, Sharru Nada led the long caravan through the gates and up the streets of the city.

Hadan Gula confided in him,

I have always hoped to become a man like my grandfather. But I never realized exactly what kind of man he was until you showed me. Now that I understand, I admire him all the more, and I am determined to be like him. I fear I can never repay you for giving me the true key to his success. From this day forward, I will use his key. I will start humbly, just as he did. That fits my true station in life far better than jewels and fine robes.

As he said this, Hadan Gula pulled the jeweled baubles from his ears and the rings from his fingers. Then, pulling the reins of his horse, he dropped back and rode with deep respect behind the leader of the caravan.
